perturbation.original_text: in connection with Adviser providing investment advisory or sub-advisory services to investment companies registered under the Investment Company Act of 1940 ("1940 Act") and/or separate accounts not required to be so registered (collectively, "Accounts"); and WHEREAS, the Adviser and FASC desire to amend the Services indicated in Exhibit A to the Services Agreement, solely with respect to Accounts that are not investment companies registered under the 1940 Act, to provide that, as part of the administration and risk management services provided by FASC, FASC may provide certain coordination of client portfolios and related fixed income trade execution implementation and administration services to Adviser when Adviser is acting as adviser or sub-adviser with respect to such Accounts.
perturbation.changed_text: in connection with Adviser providing investment advisory or sub-advisory services to investment companies registered under the Investment Company Act of 1940 ("1940 Act") and/or separate accounts not required to be so registered (collectively, "Accounts"); and WHEREAS, the Adviser and FASC desire to amend the Services indicated in Exhibit A to the Services Agreement, solely with respect to Accounts that are investment companies registered under the 1940 Act, to provide that, as part of the administration and risk management services provided by FASC, FASC may provide certain coordination of client portfolios and related fixed income trade execution implementation and administration services to Adviser when Adviser is acting as adviser or sub-adviser with respect to such Accounts.
perturbation.explanation: The amendment scope is changed from 'Accounts that are *not* investment companies' to 'Accounts that *are* investment companies'. This creates ambiguity regarding which accounts are subject to the amended services, conflicting with the original intent.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 15(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 requires specific approval processes for investment advisory contracts with registered investment companies. By altering the scope of services provided to these accounts without clear adherence to these requirements, the amendment potentially contradicts the regulatory oversight mandated by the Act, creating uncertainty about the legality of the amended service agreement. The change could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the specific requirements of 15(a) if the correct board approvals are not obtained for these services.

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15 - Contracts of advisers and underwriters U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Written contract to serve or act as investment adviser; contents It shall be unlawful for any person to serve or act as investment adviser of a registered investment company, except pursuant to a written contract, which contract, whether with such registered company or with an investment adviser of such registered company, has been approved by the vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such registered company, and— (1) precisely describes all compensation to be paid thereunder; (2) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance is specifically approved at least annually by the board of directors or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company; (3) provides, in substance, that it may be terminated at any time, without the payment of any penalty, by the board of directors of such registered company or by vote of a majority of the outstanding voting securities of such company on not more than sixty days’ written notice to the investment adviser ; and (4) provides, in substance, for its automatic termination in the event of its assignment . (b) Written contract with company for sale by principal underwriter of security of which company is issuer; contents It shall be unlawful for any principal underwriter for a registered open-end company to offer for sale, sell, or deliver after sale any security of which such company is the issuer, except pursuant to a written contract with such company, which contract— (1) shall continue in effect for a period more than two years from the date of its execution, only so long as such continuance
perturbation.justification: The original contract text specifies the amendment scope as 'Accounts that are *not* investment companies,' while the changed text alters this to 'Accounts that *are* investment companies.' This reversal directly contradicts the initial intention regarding which accounts are subject to the amended services. The Law Explanation indicates that Section 15(a) of the Investment Company Act of 1940 requires specific approval processes for investment advisory contracts. Scraped Law Snippet 2, referencing 15 U.S. Code § 80a-15, details that it is unlawful for any person to serve as an investment adviser of a registered investment company without a written contract approved by a majority vote. By changing the scope of the amendment to include investment companies without ensuring compliance with Section 15(a), the changed text creates a legal contradiction as it potentially violates the Act's requirements for advisory contracts with registered investment companies.

perturbation.original_text: Employees of Federated Advisory Services Company provide support to portfolio managers and other employees of affiliated advisers. Such services may include development of risk management programs, production of portfolio and compliance reports for clients and/or fund Boards, coordination of client portfolios and related fixed income trade execution implementation and administration, completion of required broker and custody documentation, development and documentation of operational procedures, coordination of proxy voting activities, on-site support of hardware and software, etc.
perturbation.changed_text: Employees of Federated Advisory Services Company provide support to portfolio managers and other employees of affiliated advisers. Such services *must* include development of risk management programs, production of portfolio and compliance reports for clients and/or fund Boards, coordination of client portfolios and related fixed income trade execution implementation and administration, completion of required broker and custody documentation, development and documentation of operational procedures, coordination of proxy voting activities, on-site support of hardware and software, etc.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'may include' to 'must include' alters the discretionary nature of the services provided. This creates a rigid obligation that may conflict with evolving regulatory requirements or the actual needs of the accounts.
perturbation.law_citation: 15 U.S. Code § 80b-6 - Prohibited transactions by investment advisers
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 206 of the Investment Advisers Act prohibits investment advisers from engaging in fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative practices. By mandating a fixed set of services ('must include'), the agreement might inadvertently force FASC to provide services that are unnecessary or even detrimental to a client's portfolio in certain circumstances. This inflexible requirement could be viewed as a breach of fiduciary duty and potentially constitute a prohibited transaction under the Act, especially if these mandatory services lead to suboptimal investment outcomes.

Prohibited transactions by investment advisers It shall be unlawful for any investment adviser by use of the mails or any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, directly or indirectly- (1) to employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud any client or prospective client; (2) to engage in any transaction, practice, or course of business which
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 80b-6 - Prohibited transactions by investment advisers | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 80b-6 - Prohibited transactions by investment advisers U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next It shall be unlawful for any investment adviser by use of the mails or any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce , directly or indirectly— (1) to employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud any client or prospective client; (2) to engage in any transaction, practice, or course of business which operates as a fraud or deceit upon any client or prospective client; (3) acting as principal for his own account, knowingly to sell any security to or purchase any security from a client, or acting as broker for a person other than such client, knowingly to effect any sale or purchase of any security for the account of such client, without disclosing to such client in writing before the completion of such transaction the capacity in which he is acting and obtaining the consent of the client to such transaction. The prohibitions of this paragraph shall not apply to any transaction with a customer of a broker or dealer if such broker or dealer is not acting as an investment adviser in relation to such transaction; or (4) to engage in any act, practice, or course of business which is fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative. The Commission shall, for the purposes of this paragraph (4) by rules and regulations define, and prescribe means reasonably designed to prevent, such acts, practices, and courses of business as are fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative. (Aug. 22, 1940, ch. 686, title II, § 206, 54 Stat. 852 ; Pub. L. 86–750 , §§ 8, 9, Sept. 13, 1960 , 74 Stat. 887 ; Pub. L. 111–203, title IX, § 985(e)(2) , July 21, 2010 , 124 Stat. 1935 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2010—Par. (3). Pub. L. 111–203 inserted “or” at end. 1960— Pub. L.
perturbation.justification: The change from 'may include' to 'must include' transforms a discretionary list of services into a mandatory one. According to the Law Explanation, this inflexibility could lead to providing unnecessary or detrimental services, potentially violating Section 206 of the Investment Advisers Act, which aims to prevent fraudulent, deceptive, or manipulative practices. 15 U.S. Code § 80b-6 prohibits investment advisers from engaging in any transaction, practice, or course of business which operates as a fraud or deceit upon any client. By mandating services that may not be in the client's best interest, the modified text could be interpreted as engaging in such a prohibited practice, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Amendment shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Commonwealth of Pennsylvania.
perturbation.changed_text: This Amendment shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Pennsylvania to Delaware could create conflict if certain provisions of the contract are interpreted differently under Delaware law, especially concerning statutory trusts and fiduciary duties.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Uniform Trust Act, 20 Pa.C.S. § 7701 et seq.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original agreement specifies Pennsylvania law. The Pennsylvania Uniform Trust Act (20 Pa.C.S. § 7701 et seq.) governs trusts within Pennsylvania. By switching to Delaware law, the agreement might not align with specific provisions designed to protect beneficiaries under Pennsylvania law, creating a legal contradiction and potentially weakening protections afforded under the original jurisdiction. Specifically, sections regarding trustee duties, liability, and reporting requirements may differ significantly, leading to uncertainty in enforcement.

perturbation.justification: The change from Pennsylvania to Delaware law introduces a potential contradiction. The Law Explanation indicates that Pennsylvania's Uniform Trust Act (20 Pa.C.S. § 7701 et seq.) governs trusts within Pennsylvania, offering beneficiary protections. The changed text, by applying Delaware law, may weaken these protections under the original jurisdiction, as sections regarding trustee duties, liability, and reporting requirements may differ. Scraped Law Snippet 2 shows 20 PA Cons Stat § 7701, referencing the Uniform Trust Act's short title in Pennsylvania, which would not apply under Delaware law, causing a contradiction regarding beneficiary protections.
